does: This paper addresses efficient preference alignment in large language models by formulating preference data collection as an active contextual dueling bandit problem. The authors propose an active exploration algorithm that strategically selects contexts to query for human preference feedback, rather than sampling uniformly. They prove the algorithm achieves polynomial worst-case regret bounds and extend the approach with both online and offline variants for practical LLM alignment. The method is evaluated against baselines using limited human preference samples across multiple language models and real-world datasets, demonstrating improved sample efficiency compared to passive data collection strategies.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in preference-based reinforcement learning, particularly extending recent advances in RLHF and DPO that have proven effective for aligning LLMs with human values. It connects to active learning and contextual bandit theory, which provide principled frameworks for efficient data acquisition. The paper draws on dueling bandit literature that handles preference feedback rather than scalar rewards, and combines this with active learning's insight that strategic query selection can reduce sample complexity. It responds to the practical challenge that human feedback is costly, building on prior work showing that preference alignment is crucial for safe LLM deployment while scaling to large models remains expensive.
enables: This work opens pathways for more cost-effective human-in-the-loop alignment of increasingly capable language models by reducing the preference annotations required to achieve good alignment. The theoretical regret bounds provide foundations for further algorithmic innovations in active preference learning. Future work could extend the approach to multi-annotator settings with disagreement, incorporate uncertainty quantification for preference predictions, or develop adaptive methods that adjust exploration strategies based on model-specific properties. The framework could enable broader deployment of preference-aligned models by making alignment more accessible to resource-constrained organizations, and might inform design of human feedback interfaces that focus annotation effort on maximally informative comparisons.
